coredata.dc:title: Detection of two markers for pancreatic cancer (CEA, CA199) based on a nano-silicon sphere-cyclodextrin recognition platform
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 THE AUTHORS. Published by Elsevier BV on behalf of Faculty of Engineering, Alexandria University.
coredata.prism:publisher: THE AUTHORS. Published by Elsevier BV on behalf of Faculty of Engineering, Alexandria University.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xiaohui
Meng, Ping
Li, Shuhong
Tan, Jinhua
Su, Bude
Cheng, Qi
Yang, Xiaoling
coredata.dc:description: The detection of tumor markers has significant importance in the early diagnosis of cancer. It is an important indicator for evaluating the extent of the disease and monitoring the efficacy of tumor treatment. Therefore, it is meaningful to find a convenient, fast, and sensitive method to detect tumor markers. Carbohydrate antigen 19–9 (CA19-9) is the first discovered carbohydrate antigen tumor marker with the highest sensitivity and specificity for pancreatic cancer. Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), as the most widely spectrum carbohydrate antigen tumor marker, can show an upward trend in many tumors. In this work, an electrochemical immunosensing platform was proposed for the detection of CEA and CA199. Nano-silicon sphere-cyclodextrin was synthesized for antibody capture. Graphene and gold nanoparticles were used to modify the electrode, improving the electronic conductivity of the electrode. The synthesized sensor can be used for highly sensitive detection of two tumor markers. This method also provides a new platform for clinical immune analysis of other biomolecules.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical sensor
Silicon sphere
Biomarkers
Pancreatic cancer
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of electrochemical immunosensor and detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM image of prepared nSiO2-CD with 300,000 magnifications.
FT-IR spectrum of prepared SiO2, amine-functionalized SiO2 and nSiO2-CD.

(A) The chronoamperograms of the immunosensors Ab2@199, nSiO2-Ab2@199, nSiO2-CD-Ab2@199 after the continuous addition of 5 mM H2O2 in nitrogen atmosphere PBS. (B) The chronoamperograms of the immunosensors Ab2@CEA, nSiO2-Ab2@CEA, nSiO2-CD-Ab2@CEA after the continuous addition of 5 mM H2O2 in nitrogen atmosphere PBS.
EIS of GCE, rGO/GCE, AuNPs/rGO/GCE, Ab/AuNPs/rGO/GCE, BSA/Ab1/AuNPs/rGO/GCE, CEA/BSA/Ab1/AuNPs/rGO/GCE, nSiO2-CD-Ab2/CEA/BSA/Ab1/AuNPs/rGO/GCE in 2.5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-+ 0.1 M KCl.

(A) Time-current plots of different concentrations of CEA (0.001, 0.005, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 3, 5, 8 and 12 U/mL). (B) Working curve of the immunosensor for different concentrations of CEA, showing a linear relationship between the response current and CEA concentration.

(A) Time-current plots of different concentrations of CA199 (0.01, 1, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 5, 8, 12, 16 and 20 U/mL). (B) Working curve of the immunosensor for different concentrations of CA199, illustrating a linear relationship between the response current and CA199 concentration.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Peer review under responsibility of Faculty of Engineering, Alexandria University.

head.ce:title: Detection of two markers for pancreatic cancer (CEA, CA199) based on a nano-silicon sphere-cyclodextrin recognition platform

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of General Surgery, Bayinguoleng Mengguzizhizhou People’s Hospital, Korla 841000, Xinjiang, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of General Surgery, Bayinguoleng Mengguzizhizhou People’s Hospital, Korla 841000, Xinjiang, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The detection of tumor markers has significant importance in the early diagnosis of cancer. It is an important indicator for evaluating the extent of the disease and monitoring the efficacy of tumor treatment. Therefore, it is meaningful to find a convenient, fast, and sensitive method to detect tumor markers. Carbohydrate antigen 19–9 (CA19-9) is the first discovered carbohydrate antigen tumor marker with the highest sensitivity and specificity for pancreatic cancer. Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), as the most widely spectrum carbohydrate antigen tumor marker, can show an upward trend in many tumors. In this work, an electrochemical immunosensing platform was proposed for the detection of CEA and CA199. Nano-silicon sphere-cyclodextrin was synthesized for antibody capture. Graphene and gold nanoparticles were used to modify the electrode, improving the electronic conductivity of the electrode. The synthesized sensor can be used for highly sensitive detection of two tumor markers. This method also provides a new platform for clinical immune analysis of other biomolecules.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is a leading cause of death worldwide, and early diagnosis plays a crucial role in improving patient outcomes and survival rates. Carbohydrate antigen 19–9 (CA199) and carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) are two critical serum tumor markers commonly used for the diagnosis and monitoring of various cancers, including pancreatic, colorectal, breast, and lung cancers. Accurate and sensitive detection of these markers is essential for the effective management of cancer patients [1–3]. Because the pancreas is an intra-abdominal organ, obtaining tissue for pathological examination is challenging, and diagnosis can only be confirmed through imaging studies. However, imaging studies can only reveal the disease after it has progressed to an advanced stage [4]. Therefore, finding a correct and simple diagnostic method for early-stage pancreatic cancer is crucial to improving patient survival rates, and it is also the focus of this research. This study aims to investigate the early diagnosis and staging of pancreatic cancer, as well as postoperative monitoring and prognosis prediction, using the hot topic of tumor markers, specifically carbohydrate antigen tumor markers, which are currently being studied both domestically and internationally [5–8]. This study has significant scientific significance and extensive social application prospects. Currently, for the detection of carbohydrate antigen tumor markers, relying on a single marker is insufficient for diagnosis, and lacks both sensitivity and specificity [9]. From the clinical detection combination of several commonly used carbohydrate antigen tumor markers for colon, ovarian, and breast cancers, both domestic and foreign research trends suggest that the combination of multiple markers can improve the relevance of tumor diagnosis [10].

Tumor markers refer to a class of substances secreted or produced by tumor cells during their occurrence and proliferation, which are released into cells, blood, or body fluids, and can reflect the presence and growth of tumors [11,12]. By measuring the levels of serum tumor markers, relevant information regarding the early diagnosis, evaluation of prognosis, efficacy observation, and recurrence of malignant tumors can be obtained. Currently, more than 200 types of tumor markers have been discovered, of which over 40 are commonly used and can be classified into four types: carbohydrate antigen, oncogene, enzyme, and isoenzyme. Carbohydrate antigen tumor markers are the most extensively applied tumor markers and the most intensively studied in clinical practice. These tumor markers refer to antigens on the surface of tumor cells or substances produced by tumor cells that contain carbohydrate components, including glycolipids and glycoproteins [13–15]. Their antigenic determinants can be located on nuclear proteins or glycans. The commonly used carbohydrate markers for the clinical diagnosis of various malignant tumors include CA50, CEA, CA15-3, CA12-5, CA19-9, CA19-5, CA72-4, CA242, CA549, CA27, and CA29. Current research has shown that certain combinations of carbohydrate antigen tumor markers are highly correlated with specific types of cancer, such as the combination of CA54-9, CA15-3, CA27, and CA29 with breast cancer, the combination of CA50 and CA1250 with ovarian cancer, and the combination of CA199, CA242, and CA724 with colorectal cancer [16–18]. The nine carbohydrate antigen tumor markers mentioned above also have a certain correlation with pancreatic cancer, but so far, no exact carbohydrate antigen tumor markers for pancreatic cancer have been identified. CA199 is a high molecular weight glycoprotein obtained through monoclonal antibody separation [19]. It is the most sensitive and specific carbohydrate antigen tumor marker discovered for pancreatic cancer, with a significant increase in the serum CA199 levels of 83% to 93% of pancreatic cancer patients. CA199 is not only a marker for pancreatic cancer, but also shows significant increases in other organ malignancies, such as pancreatic cancer, gastric cancer, lung adenocarcinoma, breast cancer, and colorectal cancer. CEA is an embryonic carcinoma antigen and the most widely used carbohydrate antigen tumor marker, which exhibits an increasing trend in many tumors [20,21]. CEA elevation can be observed in malignant tumors of the digestive and non-digestive systems, and when liver metastases occur, the increase in CEA is even more significant. Since its first reported use in 1979, CEA has gradually become one of the most commonly used markers for pancreatic cancer and one of the most popular participating projects in multiple tumor marker combinations.

Currently, the immunological methods for detecting tumor markers in serum in clinical settings mainly include enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), radioimmunoassay (RIA), chemiluminescent immunoassay (CLIA), immunoluminometric assay (ILMA), immunofluorescence assay (IFA), and time-resolved fluorescence immunoassay (TRFIA) [22–24]. Although these methods have the advantages of high sensitivity, accuracy, and specificity, they have various shortcomings in environmental protection, operational speed, quantitative detection, and other aspects. Although some of them have been automated, many of them cannot be widely used in laboratories due to the expensive price of specialized instruments and reagents. Therefore, developing new immune detection technologies to reduce testing costs, accelerate testing speed and online capability to meet the needs of large-scale screening has become an unresolved issue [25–27]. Recently, immune sensors have been widely used in the detection of tumor markers.

Immuno-sensors are a novel type of biosensor that couples a biologically sensitive membrane containing antigen–antibody molecules with a signal transducer. Immuno-sensors combine high-sensitivity sensing technology and highly specific immune reactions, providing real-time output, high sensitivity, strong specificity, simple operation, and low cost [28]. Moreover, because immuno-sensors can monitor the antigen–antibody reaction on the sensor surface in real time, they are conducive to kinetic analysis of immune reactions. Therefore, the development of immuno-sensors can promote the development of immune diagnostic methods towards quantification and automation [29]. This work constructed an electrochemical immuno-sensor based on nano-silicon sphere-cyclodextrin (nSiO2-CD) as a label for detecting the pancreatic cancer biomarkers CA199 and CEA. An electrode modified with reducible oxidized graphene (rGO) and gold nanoparticles was used to increase the electrode's specific surface area, capture a large amount of the first antibody (Ab1), and improve the electron transfer rate. Using nSiO2-CD as the labeling material not only has a large specific surface area for adsorbing more secondary antibodies but also has strong catalytic activity for hydrogen peroxide, thereby amplifying the electrochemical signal and making the immuno-sensor highly sensitive with a low detection limit. This strategy can provide a new platform for clinical immunoassays of other biomolecules.

Analytical grade cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTS), β-CD, pyridine, and dimethylformamide (DMF) were used without any additional treatment. CA199, CEA, anti-CA199 antibodies, and anti-CEA antibodies were purchased from Shanghai Leading Biotechnology Co., Ltd. rGO was acquired from Nanjing Pioneer Material Science and Technology Co., Ltd. Bovine serum albumin (BSA) and HAuCl·3HO were both procured from Sigma-Aldrich (China, Beijing) corporation.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) analysis was carried out using a Perkin-Elmer Spectrum GX FT-IR spectrometer with KBr pellets and the spectra were measured over the range of 550 to 4000 cm−1. The morphology of the nanoparticles was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images. An electrochemical workstation (CHI760D) obtained from Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd. was utilized for electrochemical measurements in the experiments.

Silica nanoparticles were synthesized through the sol–gel method. Initially, a specific quantity of CTAB was added to 100 mL of de-ionized water and subjected to stirring. After sonication by prob sonicator for 5 min, 1 mL of NH3 solution and 2 mL of TEOS were added dropwise into the solution. The mixture was then centrifuged and washed with water and ethanol several times to eliminate any unreacted materials. The final product was dried at room temperature. Subsequently, the surfactant template was removed by placing the white precipitate in a furnace at 400 °C for 2 h. Next, 1 g of the synthesized nanoparticles was mixed with 1.5 mL of APTS and 50 mL of toluene, and the mixture was refluxed for 3 h. The obtained product was filtered and washed with toluene, and then placed in an oven at 100 °C. The precipitate obtained was then mixed with 60 mL of water and stirred for 1 h, and subsequently dried at 100 °C to eliminate any unreacted APTS. For the production of nSiO2-CD, 1.5 g of amino-functionalized silica nanoparticle was dispersed in 30 mL of DMF at room temperature. A solution of 0.2 g of β-CD and 5 mL of DMF was then added, and the mixture was refluxed under a nitrogen atmosphere at 40 °C. The resulting product was subjected to centrifugation and washed with DMF and acetone repeatedly. Finally, the product was dried at room temperature.

Accurately weigh 0.50 g of PVP and add it to a three-necked flask. Add 10 mL of ultrapure water to the flask and vigorously stir the solution. Then, add 15 mL of 0.1 M ascorbic acid and 2.5 mL of 9.5 mM HAuCl4 to the solution, and stir continuously at room temperature for 30 min. The resulting product is then centrifuged and washed three times with ultrapure water and ethanol. The obtained AuNPs are dispersed in 10 mL of ultrapure water and kept aside for further use.

1 mL of 10 μg/mL concentration of either anti-CA199 or anti-CEA was added to 2 mg of nSiO2-CD and mixed thoroughly. The mixture was shaken for 24 h at 4℃ in an incubator, then separated by centrifugation in a refrigerated centrifuge. The supernatant was removed, followed by the addition of 1 mL of pH 7.4 phosphate buffer. The resulting product was nSiO2-CD-Ab2@199 or nSiO2-CD-Ab2@CEA and was stored in a refrigerator at 4℃.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of electrochemical immunosensor and detection
ce:section.ce:para: To start with, polish a 3 mm-diameter glassy carbon electrode using 1.0, 0.3, and 0.05 μm alumina polishing powder sequentially, followed by cleaning with ethanol through ultrasonication and rinsing with ultrapure water. Then, construct the electrode layer by layer by modifying it with an rGO solution (1 mg/mL), AuNPs (10 mg/L), Ab1@199 (or CEA), 1 wt% BSA solution, different concentrations of CA199 (or CEA), and the prepared labeling material solution (nSiO2-CD-Ab2@199 or nSiO2-CD-Ab2@CEA), and after each layer is fixed, wash it with ultrapure water to remove any unbound substance. Finally, store the successfully constructed electrode for later use under 4℃ conditions.

In this experiment, the current of the electrode was measured using a conventional three-electrode system: a modified glassy carbon electrode was used as the working electrode (with a diameter of 3 mm), a platinum electrode as the auxiliary electrode, and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode. Electrode impedance characterization was performed in a testing solution containing 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- and 0.1 M KCI. The electrode performance test was conducted in a 5 mL PBS buffer solution at pH 7.4 (with 0.1 M KCI as a supporting electrolyte), using cyclic voltammetry to explore the response performance of the electrode. Unless otherwise specified, the scanning temperature during the CV detection process was room temperature, the potential scan range was −0.6 to + 0.6 V, and the potential scan rate was 100 mV/s. Chronoamperometry was used to measure the current at a potential of −0.5 V. After the background current was stable, 5.0 mM H2O2 was injected into the PBS solution and the current change value was recorded.

SEM was utilized to examine the morphology of the nanoparticles that were produced. The SEM images of the nanoparticles are illustrated in Fig. 1 , and display uniform spherical nanoparticles with a radius of less than 100 nm, as seen in both the low and high magnification images.

The FTIR spectra of SiO2, amine-functionalized SiO2, and nSiO2-CD were illustrated in Fig. 2 . The wide peak within the IR absorption band between 3000 and 3500 cm−1 was caused by the stretching vibration of O–H and N–H bonds. The two asymmetric and symmetric peaks located at 1091 cm − 1 and 814 cm−1 were assigned to the vibration of Si–O–Si [30]. The N–H group bonding vibration was detected at 1630 cm−1, and the N–H stretching was detected at 3443 cm−1. Based on these results, we prepared amino-functionalized silica nanoparticles. The spectrum of nSiO2-CD had two peaks at 1391 and 1661 cm−1, which indicated that the cross-linked structure of β-CD was present on the silica nanoparticles [31]. In summary, these results suggest that the grafting of β-CD onto silica nanoparticle hollow spheres was successful.

To improve the sensitivity of the sandwich-type immunosensor, the focus is mainly on the secondary antibody labeling. In order to compare, we prepared Ab2@199, nSiO2-Ab2@199, nSiO2-CD-Ab2@199, Ab2@CEA, nSiO2-CD-Ab2@CEA, nSiO2-CD-Ab2@CEA for detecting the current response of the immunosensor to catalyzing H2O2 when detecting 10 U/mL CA199 and CEA [32]. As shown in Fig. 3 , the experimental results demonstrate that the immunosensor labelled with Au-Ab as the secondary antibody has a small current response after continuously adding 5 mM H2O2. The immunosensor labelled with Au@nSiO2-Ab2 as the secondary antibody has a larger current response after continuously adding 5 mM H2O2, while the immunosensor labelled with Au@nSiO2-CD-Ab2 shows the highest current change. Therefore, selecting nSiO2-CD as the labelling material is superior to the immunosensors prepared with AuNPs or nSiO2 as the secondary antibody labelling material in terms of the current catalytic response to H2O2.

The EIS spectrum is a characterization of the electrode interface structure during the electrode assembly process. The impedance spectra of the CEA sensor in a 2.5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-+ 0.1 M KCI solution during the assembly process are represented by the curves in Fig. 4 . As shown in Fig. 4, the impedance changes with the surface modification of the electrode. The semi-circular diameter of the electrode modified with a layer of rGO on the bare glassy carbon electrode is smaller than that of the bare electrode because reduced rGO has good conductivity and excellent electron transfer capabilities, displaying corresponding electrochemical properties [33]. The semi-circular diameter of the graph decreases significantly when gold nanoparticles are further modified onto the electrode, indicating that the impedance value becomes smaller, which means that gold nanoparticles can accelerate electron transfer. When Ab1 is fixed onto the surface of the electrode, the impedance value of the AuNPs/rGO/Ab1/GCE-modified electrode increases because the antibody is a biopolymer that is not conducive to electron transfer, hindering the transfer of electrons [34]. Then, after adding BSA to block nonspecific binding sites on the electrode, the impedance value of the modified electrode further increases. When the antigen CEA binds to the antibody on the electrode surface, the electrode impedance value further increases [35]. The electrode impedance continues to increase after labelling, which is due to the fact that proteins are non-active electrochemical substances that hinder electron transfer. These results demonstrate that the sensor modification process is successful.

We utilized chronoamperometry to evaluate the response characteristics of electrochemical immunosensors with different concentrations of CEA, and the current change curve with the concentration change is presented in Fig. 5 . In the range of CEA concentration from 0.001 to 12 U/mL, the immunoelectrode exhibits good linear response, and the CEA concentration is linearly related to the response current, with a detection limit of 0.0005 U/mL.

We employed chronoamperometry to evaluate the response performance of electrochemical immunosensors for different concentrations of CA199, and the current change with the concentration change curve is shown in Fig. 6 . The immunoelectrode has a good linear response in the range of 0.05–20 U/mL for CA199 concentration, and the concentration of CA199 has a linear relationship with the response current. The detection limit is 0.01 U/mL.

To assess the practical performance of the immunosensor, reproducibility is an important indicator. In order to evaluate the reproducibility of the immunosensor, five electrodes prepared from the same batch were used to detect the same concentration of CEA and CA199, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated. The current response of the five electrodes to the same concentration of CA199 was measured and an RSD of 2.4% was obtained. To evaluate the stability of the immunosensor, we measured the current response of the same immunosensor for 0.1 U/mL CEA and 1 U/mL CA199 after storing it at 4 °C for different time intervals (1, 7, 14, and 21 days). The current response decreased by 5.6% for CEA and 4.8% for CA199 after 7 days, and by 11.2% for CEA and 9.6% for CA199 after 21 days. These results indicate that the immunosensor has acceptable stability for practical applications, although a decline in sensitivity is observed over time.

To assess the potential interference from amino acids and vitamins on the immunosensor's performance, we tested the response of the immunosensor in the presence of common interfering substances, such as ascorbic acid, uric acid, glutamic acid, cysteine, and folic acid. The immunosensor was exposed to 0.1 U/mL CEA and 1 U/mL CA199, along with various concentrations of the interfering substances. The current response changes were less than 5% in the presence of these substances, indicating that the immunosensor exhibits good selectivity and is not significantly affected by the presence of common amino acids and vitamins.

To evaluate the performance of our immunosensor in real sample analysis, we tested serum samples from patients with different concentrations of CEA and CA199. The serum samples were obtained from a local hospital, and the concentrations of CEA and CA199 were determined using a standard ELISA as a reference method. The samples were diluted to fit within the linear range of our immunosensor, and the results obtained using our immunosensor were compared with those obtained using ELISA. The recovery rates of our immunosensor for CEA and CA199 in real samples were within the range of 95–105%, demonstrating its accuracy and reliability for detecting these tumor markers in real samples. Moreover, there was a strong correlation between the results obtained using our immunosensor and the reference ELISA method, indicating the potential of our immunosensor for clinical applications in detecting CEA and CA199 in patient serum samples.

This study developed an electrochemical immunosensor for detecting CA199 and CEA, using nSiO2-CD as a marker. To enhance the sensor's performance, a rGO and gold nanoparticles modified electrode was employed to increase its specific surface area, allowing for the capture of a large number of Ab1 and facilitating electron transfer. The use of nSiO2-CD as a marker material not only provided a large surface area for adsorbing more Ab2, but also exhibited strong catalytic activity towards hydrogen peroxide, amplifying the electrochemical signal and increasing the immunosensor's sensitivity and lower detection limit.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
